clean_story: Numbers with rules.  Math that can’t answer everything. "The limits of math" refers to the boundaries where even perfect equations hit walls—like a ruler trying to measure a shadow. Math solves puzzles with absolute precision, yet some questions slip through its fingers. Imagine a calculator that works flawlessly until you ask, "What’s the taste of pi?"—it wasn’t built for that. One famous limit:Kurt Gödel proved in 1931 that in any system complex enough to include arithmetic, there are true statements that can’t be proven true within that system. Like a locked box with the key inside—you see it, but the rules won’t let you open it. Another example:The "halting problem" shows no algorithm can predict whether a program will run forever or stop. It’s like asking, "Will this hourglass ever empty?" while blindfolded—math can’t peek inside. Even physics hits these walls. Black holes warp space so severely that equations spit out "infinity," a sign they’ve failed—not that reality is infinite. Math describes the universe’s behavior, not its why. Three related subjects are unknowable truths, paradox engines, and formal systems.
